items.hook: Happy to help you narrow it down without making it complicated.
items.bridge: At a coffee shop, the easiest useful split is usually black or milk-based, hot or iced, and smooth or stronger flavor.
items.next_step: Ask which style they lean toward and whether they want less bitterness.
items.handoff_suggestion: Menu-guidance opportunity if the user replies with preference details.

items.hook: That is a very workable place to start.
items.bridge: If someone wants coffee flavor without too much bite, milk balance and roast feel matter more than a generic bestseller list.
items.next_step: Ask whether they want hot or iced and how coffee-forward they want it.
items.handoff_suggestion: Good engagement signal if the user answers with taste details.

items.hook: I get why you want to avoid waiting around.
items.bridge: Ready time can swing with store traffic, order size, and whether it is pickup or delivery.
items.next_step: Ask for the store, order mode, and drink count.
items.handoff_suggestion: Send to a live store or Catcher path quickly.

items.hook: Makes sense to check before you carry your laptop over.
items.bridge: Seats, outlets, and noise level can change a lot by branch and time of day.
items.next_step: Ask which store, what time, and how many people are coming.
items.handoff_suggestion: Route as a live availability check if the user gives fixed details.

items.hook: A coffee meeting usually works better when the basics are lined up first.
items.bridge: Group size, arrival time, and whether you need a quieter corner all shape the right branch choice.
items.next_step: Ask for branch, time, group size, and whether outlets matter.
items.handoff_suggestion: Business-meeting lead if details are already concrete.

items.hook: Good call to check before you make the trip.
items.bridge: Seasonal drinks and fresh pastry batches can vary by store and timing more than people expect.
items.next_step: Ask for the store and whether they plan to order now or later today.

items.hook: That sounds like a real order, not casual browsing.
items.bridge: For a multi-cup coffee run, the key details are usually store, timing, drink count, and delivery versus pickup.
items.next_step: Ask for store, ready time, drink count, and whether invoice is needed.
items.handoff_suggestion: Route as a high-intent operational lead.

items.hook: Morning orders are much easier when the timing is clear upfront.
items.bridge: Pastries and breakfast sets often depend on the store and pickup window, not just a quick yes or no.
items.next_step: Ask which store, what food items, and what pickup time they need.
items.handoff_suggestion: Store-confirmation path for timed food orders.

items.hook: That already sounds close to a workable booking.
items.bridge: Once invoice needs and timing are involved, the order is really about logistics rather than a generic menu question.
items.next_step: Ask for store, timing, drink count, and invoice details.

items.hook: I am glad you asked before deciding on the drink.
items.bridge: Questions about caffeine or suitability depend on the exact drink and what the person is sensitive to.
items.next_step: Ask for the exact drink and what they are trying to avoid.

items.hook: That is the kind of detail worth checking carefully.
items.bridge: Milk alternatives, toppings, and kitchen handling should be confirmed for the exact drink instead of guessed.
items.next_step: Ask for the allergen or ingredient concern, the drink, and the store.

items.bridge: With coffee, people often care about different proof points like roast freshness, bean origin, milk balance, or how sweet the drink tastes.
items.next_step: Invite the user to name the exact quality point they doubt.

items.hook: Makes sense to check the offer before you place the order.
items.bridge: Member perks and coupons often change by store, channel, and time window.
items.next_step: Ask for the store and whether they are ordering in app, in person, or by delivery.
items.handoff_suggestion: Useful bridge into a real order if the user confirms details.

items.hook: I can understand why that would feel frustrating.
items.bridge: The fastest way to check a drink issue is usually the store, order time, and a photo if there is one.
items.next_step: Move the user to DM for order details and photos.
items.handoff_suggestion: Escalate to after-sales or store operations.

items.hook: That kind of coffee setup can work well when it is planned early enough.
items.bridge: Event requests usually depend on date, location, drink count, and whether you need branding or just delivery timing.
items.next_step: Ask for date, city or store, drink count, and the exact setup they want.

items.hook: Happy to move this forward quickly.
items.bridge: The safest first step is still platform DM so order details do not get scattered in public comments.
items.next_step: Ask for the store plus order details in DM.
